{"notes": [{"url": "https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08266v1", "title": "Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with High ... - arXiv", "summary": "Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with High-Confidence Safety Constraints Abstract Existing approaches to language model alignment often treat safety as a tradeoff against helpfulness, which can lead to unacceptable responses in sensitive domains. To ensure reliable performance in such settings, we propose High-Confidence Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (HC-RLHF), a method that provides high-confidence safety guarantees while maximizing helpfulness. Similar to previous methods, HC-RLHF explicitly decouples human preferences into helpfulness and harmlessness (safety), which are learned by training a reward model and a cost model, respectively.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["It is therefore essential that LLMs generate outputs that are both helpful and safe, and avoid harms such as misinformation, toxicity, or abetting of dangerous activities (Gehman et al., 2020; Weidinger et al., 2021; Ganguli et al., 2022).", "However, these goals of helpfulness and harmlessness often conflict, such as when the user asks for help with a potentially harmful activity (Glaese et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022a).", "Recent work addresses these challenges by decoupling human preference data into separate helpfulness and harmlessness objectives and enforcing harmlessness as a safety constraint—an approach called Safe RLHF (Dai et al., 2023).", "In this work, we propose High-Confidence Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (HC-RLHF), which leverages the Seldonian framework (Thomas et al., 2019) to enforce probabilistic guarantees on harmlessness.", "Empirically, we fine-tuned the Qwen2-1.5B (Yang et al., 2024), Llama3.2-3b (Grattafiori et al., 2024), and Qwen2.5-3b (Qwen et al., 2025) models using HC-RLHF."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: It is therefore essential that LLMs generate outputs that are both helpful and safe, and avoid harms such as misinformat"}, {"url": "https://rlj.cs.umass.edu/2025/papers/RLJ_RLC_2025_62.pdf", "title": "[PDF] Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with High ...", "summary": "∣ ∣ ## Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with High-Confidence Safety Constraints Yaswanth Chittepu , Blossom Metevier , Will Schwarzer , Austin Hoag , Scott Niekum , Philip S. Thomas Keywords: Language model alignment, Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), Safe reinforcement learning, AI safety ## Summary Existing approaches to language model alignment often treat safety as a tradeoff against helpfulness, which can lead to unacceptable responses in sensitive domains. To ensure reliable performance in such settings, we propose High-Confidence Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (HC-RLHF), a method that provides high-confidence safety guarantees while maximizing helpfulness.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["We introduce HC-RLHF, the first Seldonian algorithm (Thomas et al., 2019) with applications to RLHF.", "With high probability, HC-RLHF can find solutions that satisfy the safety constraint introduced by Safe RLHF (Dai et al., 2023). 2.", "Context: HC-RLHF builds on two works: Safe RLHF (Dai et al., 2023) and the Seldonian framework (Thomas et al., 2019).", "Context: This ensures that HC-RLHF is indeed a Seldonian algorithm (Thomas et al., 4. 2019). 3.", "Empirically, we apply HC-RLHF to align three different language models (Qwen2-1.5B, Qwen2.5-3B, and LLaMa-3.2-3B) with human preferences."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: We introduce HC-RLHF, the first Seldonian algorithm (Thomas et al., 2019) with applications to RLHF."}, {"url": "https://blog.bluedot.org/p/rlhf-limitations-for-ai-safety", "title": "Problems with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback ...", "summary": "Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the primary technique currently used to align the outputs of Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences. We have RLHF to thank for the fact that current LLMs produce helpful, humanlike text that largely avoids harmful or inappropriate responses. However, this has led some to mistakenly believe that it will be sufficient to address safety concerns from ever-more-powerful models.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["We have RLHF to thank for the fact that current LLMs produce helpful, humanlike text that largely avoids harmful or inappropriate responses.", "First, the untuned or ‘base’ model is tested in a controlled environment.", "This data is presented to human annotators, who provide feedback, most commonly by ranking the model’s outputs, labelling them as either ‘good’ or ‘bad’, or choosing between two answers to the same prompt.", "It acts to steer the agent’s behaviour by positively or negatively rewarding its outputs according to how well they align with the data it has collected on human preferences.", "This can lead to a preference for outputs that match user beliefs over truthful ones, or for courses of action that prioritise a user’s short-term approval over their true interests."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: We have RLHF to thank for the fact that current LLMs produce helpful, humanlike text that largely avoids harmful or inap"}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12137480/", "title": "Helpful, harmless, honest? Sociotechnical limits of AI alignment and ...", "summary": "Abstract This paper critically evaluates the attempts to align Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, especially Large Language Models (LLMs), with human values and intentions through Reinforcement Learning from Feedback methods, involving either human feedback (RLHF) or AI feedback (RLAIF). Specifically, we show the shortcomings of the broadly pursued alignment goals of honesty, harmlessness, and helpfulness. Through a multidisciplinary sociotechnical critique, we examine both the theoretical underpinnings and practical implementations of RLHF techniques, revealing significant limitations in their approach to capturing the complexities of human ethics, and contributing to AI safety.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Abstract This paper critically evaluates the attempts to align Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, especially Large Language Models (LLMs), with human values and intentions through Reinforcement Learning from Feedback methods, involving either huma", "It is often claimed by AI companies and researchers that RLHF fine-tuning ensures that the LLMs they market and sell conform (or ‘align’) to human values, in particular by responding in ways that are ‘helpful’, ‘harmless’, and ‘honest’ (the HHH princ", "First introduced by Askell et al. (2021), the HHH principle has become widely adopted in LLM alignment practices (Kamath et al., 2024).", "We analyse ethical issues introduced or made worse by the use of those techniques in The Internal Tensions and Ethical Issues in RLHF.", "Background LLMs are generative models that predict subsequent tokens, or words, when given a sequence of words as input."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Abstract This paper critically evaluates the attempts to align Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, especially Large La"}, {"url": "https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3743127", "title": "RLHF Deciphered: A Critical Analysis of Reinforcement Learning ...", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT", "perspectives": [], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": ""}, {"url": "https://www.reddit.com/r/artificial/comments/1r223lp/rlhf_safety_training_enforces_what_ai_can_say/", "title": "RLHF safety training enforces what AI can say about itself ... - Reddit", "summary": "RLHF safety training enforces what AI can say about itself, not what it can do — experimental evidence 人氣 (預設) 打開留言排序選項 最佳 人氣 (預設) 最新 爭議 舊 問答 留言區 This maps pretty cleanly to the reward hacking problem we've seen in RL for years. RLHF optimizes for human evaluator approval, and evaluators can only judge surface-level behavior—the model's expressed outputs. They can't directly observe internal reasoning or capability limits.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["They can't directly observe internal reasoning or capability limits.", "If RLHF mostly teaches the model what it is allowed to claim about itself, you can still test capability with tasks where self narration is irrelevant like tool use success rates, hidden unit tests, sandboxed actions with an audit log, and pass or fa", "A 300-400 word deep analysis focused on findings relevant to the research question, including:
   - Key data points, statistics, and numerical findings
   - Methodology or approach described (if any)
   - Contradictions or tensions with commonly know", "Which STORM perspectives are covered (from: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends)
3. 3-5 specific facts with numbers, dates, or named entities
4.", "Any data tables, statistics, or quantitative results (preserve exact numbers)
5."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: They can't directly observe internal reasoning or capability limits."}]}
